1: "Go up to Bethel and settle there, and build an altar there to God, who appeared to you when you were fleeing from your brother Esau" — YHWH's command is the fulfillment of Jacob's Bethel vow (Genesis 28:20-22): twenty years later, Jacob is directed to return and complete what he promised. The divine reminder of the original theophany ("when you were fleeing from your brother Esau") connects the current command to the entire arc of Jacob's life. The urgency of the command — given immediately after the Shechem crisis — suggests that the altar at Bethel is the proper resolution to the household's religious and social disorder.
2: "Get rid of the foreign gods you have with you, and purify yourselves and change your clothes" — the household purification before approaching Bethel involves three actions: removal of foreign gods (ʾĕlōhê hannēkhār, gods of foreigners/strangers), ritual purification (hiṭṭahărû), and change of garments. Foreign gods in the household included Rachel's teraphim (Genesis 31:19, 34-35) and possibly others acquired during the Paddan-Aram period. Change of garments was an ANE purification ritual marking transition between profane and sacred states — worn, travel-stained clothing was replaced before approaching a deity's sanctuary. The threefold preparation mirrors Sinai preparations (Exodus 19:10-14).
3: "Who answered me in the day of my distress and who has been with me wherever I have gone" — Jacob's summary of YHWH's character in the theophany-fulfillment is a covenant-fidelity formula: the God who answered (ʿānānî) when Jacob called, and who accompanied (hāyāh ʿimmāḏî, was with me) throughout the years in Haran. "The day of my distress" (bĕyôm ṣārātî) refers to the Bethel night — the flight from Esau — which is the same event YHWH referenced in Genesis 35:1. Jacob frames the altar as the response to a fulfilled covenant, not an obligation fulfilled under compulsion.
4: "They gave Jacob all the foreign gods they had and the rings in their ears, and Jacob buried them under the oak at Shechem" — the earrings (nĕzāmîm) are buried alongside the idols, indicating they functioned as religious objects — amulets, charms, or ornaments associated with Mesopotamian deities. In the ANE, jewelry was often votive or apotropaic (protective against evil); earrings bearing divine images or associated with fertility goddesses were common. The oak at Shechem — already significant as the location where Abraham received the covenant promise (Genesis 12:6: "the great tree of Moreh at Shechem") — becomes a burial site for the covenant household's foreign religious objects, a repudiation enacted at the same ground where Abraham's covenant was first announced.
5: "The terror of God fell on the towns all around them so that no one pursued them" — ḥittat ʾĕlōhîm (terror/dread of God) is the divine protection mechanism for a vulnerable journey. The same concept appears in Exodus 23:27 ("I will send my terror ahead of you and throw into confusion every nation you encounter") and in the Song of Moses (Exodus 15:14-16, where surrounding peoples are seized with dread at YHWH's power). Jacob's household was at maximum vulnerability — the Shechem massacre had made them potential targets for regional coalition retaliation. The divine terror prevents any retaliatory pursuit.
6: "Jacob and all the people with him came to Luz (that is, Bethel) in the land of Canaan" — the narrator uses both names (Luz = the pre-Israelite name; Bethel = Jacob's sacred name from Genesis 28) to establish continuity between the sites. The return to Bethel closes the narrative arc that began with Jacob's flight in Genesis 28:10. The household that left Canaan as a single man with a staff (Genesis 32:10) returns as a large household with wives, children, servants, and flocks — the fulfillment of the covenant promises enacted in the twenty years of absence.
7: "He called the place El Bethel" — the altar name "God of Bethel" (ʾēl bêt-ʾēl) is a divine epithet derived from the location. The same title appears in Hosea 12:4 (where YHWH reminds Israel of the Bethel encounter). The altar naming continues the Genesis pattern of commemorative naming at sites of divine encounter (Genesis 12:8; Genesis 13:18; Genesis 21:33; Genesis 26:25); each altar marks a place where the covenant was actualized in history.
8: "Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, died and was buried under the oak outside Bethel. So it was named Allon Bakuth" — Deborah was last mentioned traveling with Rebekah from Haran (Genesis 24:59); now, decades later, she dies and is buried under an oak beside Bethel. How she came to be with Jacob's household is not explained — possibly Rebekah had died and Deborah passed to Jacob's care, or she had been with the household throughout. The burial name ʾallôn bākhût (oak of weeping) is unique: servants were rarely memorialized by place-name in ANE narrative. The tenderness of the grief expressed signals that Deborah was a significant household presence whose loss was genuinely mourned.
9: "After Jacob returned from Paddan Aram, God appeared to him again and blessed him" — the second Bethel theophany formally closes the Paddan-Aram cycle. The "again" (ʿôḏ) connects this appearance to the first Bethel theophany (Genesis 28:13-22). The blessing is the divine action that ratifies the covenant renewal — the same pattern of theophany → blessing → covenant promises → naming that structured the original encounter.
10: "Your name is Jacob, but you will no longer be called Jacob; your name will be Israel. So he named him Israel" — the official divine renaming formalizes what the angel announced at Peniel (Genesis 32:28). In the ANE, a divine renaming was a covenant act that redefined the person's identity and role — Abram to Abraham (Genesis 17:5), Sarai to Sarah (Genesis 17:15). The formal Bethel renaming gives Israel its covenant identity in the precise location where the covenant was first announced to Jacob; it is the theological completion of the arc from 28:13 to Genesis 35:10.
11: "I am God Almighty; be fruitful and increase in number. A nation and a community of nations will come from you, and kings will be among your descendants" — ʾEl-Šaddai (God Almighty) is the covenant name used in Abraham's covenant establishment (Genesis 17:1) and Isaac's blessing of Jacob for his journey (Genesis 28:3). Its use here at Bethel ties the three patriarchs' covenant encounters together under the same divine name. "A community of nations" (qĕhal gôyîm) and "kings" among the descendants are the covenant expansions — the tribal and royal dimensions that the twelve-tribe nation and the Davidic monarchy will fulfill.
12: "The land I gave to Abraham and Isaac I also give to you, and I will give this land to your descendants after you" — the land promise is renewed for the third generation. The explicit linking of all three patriarchs ("I gave to Abraham and Isaac") makes Jacob's covenant standing historically cumulative — he does not receive a new covenant but the same covenant confirmed through three generations. The word ʾetten (I give) is in the perfect tense in some manuscripts, indicating a completed gift; in others, imperfect — reflecting the ongoing and future nature of the promise.
13: "Then God went up from him at the place where he had talked with him" — the divine departure marks the end of the theophany. "Went up" (wayyaʿal) reflects the vertical cosmology of ANE divine encounters: God descends to meet humans and returns to the divine realm above. The phrase is used for YHWH's departure in Exodus 24:18 (Moses ascending to God) and in Elijah's context; it marks the formal close of a divine-human encounter rather than a simple end to the conversation.
14: "Jacob set up a stone pillar at the place where God had talked with him, and he poured out a drink offering on it; he also poured oil on it" — the pillar (maṣṣēḇāh) echoes the one Jacob set up at the original Bethel theophany (Genesis 28:18), when he anointed it with oil. Here a drink offering (nesekh) is added — the first mention of a drink offering in Genesis. Drink offerings (wine poured on the altar) became a standard component of the Mosaic worship system (Numbers 15:5-10); Jacob's addition here prefigures that practice. The pillar + oil + drink offering is a fuller ceremonial marking than the original simple anointing.
15: "Jacob called the place where God had talked with him Bethel" — the naming confirms Genesis 28:19 (where Jacob first called it Bethel) and functions as a renewed dedication of the name: the place is now doubly named Bethel, by the young man fleeing his brother and by the returning patriarch. Bethel will remain a major cultic site in Israelite history: the ark of the covenant is kept there during the period of judges (Judges 20:26-28); Jeroboam later installs a golden calf there (1 Kings 12:29), and the prophets condemn it (Hosea 4:15; Amos 4:4; Genesis 5:5).
16: "They moved on from Bethel. While they were still some distance from Ephrath, Rachel began to give birth and had great difficulty" — Ephrath (later identified as Bethlehem, Genesis 35:19) is in the central highlands south of Jerusalem. The journey from Bethel to Ephrath is approximately 10-15 miles through difficult terrain. Rachel's labor begins mid-journey — the most vulnerable possible time for a difficult birth in the ancient world, far from settled shelter. "Great difficulty" (wattĕqaš bĕliḏtāh) indicates a complicated, prolonged labor — the kind that threatened both mother and child.
17: "The midwife said to her, 'Don't despair, for you have another son'" — midwives (mĕyalleḏet, the one who brings forth) were a recognized professional category in the ANE. Egyptian papyri and Mesopotamian texts describe midwives' roles; Exodus 1:15-21 names two Hebrew midwives (Shiphrah and Puah). The midwife's role included encouragement during labor — "don't despair" (ʾal-tîrĕʾî, don't be afraid) is also the formula used elsewhere in Genesis for divine reassurance (Genesis 15:1; Genesis 21:17; Genesis 26:24). The announcement of "another son" was important news in an era when son-bearing defined a wife's social standing; for Rachel, who prayed for children throughout Genesis 29-30, the news is simultaneously fulfilling and final.
18: "As she breathed her last — for she was dying — she named her son Ben-Oni. But his father named him Benjamin" — Rachel's naming of Ben-Oni (ben-ʾônî, son of my sorrow/suffering) is her dying act. The name encodes her experience: this birth cost her life. Jacob immediately overrides the name with Benjamin (binyāmîn): either "son of the right hand" (the right hand being the honored or powerful side — cf. the blessing position in Genesis 48:14) or "son of the south" (yāmîn as south, since Benjamin's territory is in the south of the later tribal allocation). Jacob's renaming is a paternal assertion of authority over the child's identity — he refuses to let Rachel's death-grief define the child's name and destiny.
19: "Rachel died and was buried on the way to Ephrath (that is, Bethlehem)" — Rachel's burial "on the way" (bĕderekh) rather than in the family burial cave at Machpelah is a significant departure from the patriarchal burial pattern. Jeremiah 31:15 memorializes this location: "A voice is heard in Ramah, weeping and great mourning, Rachel weeping for her children." The "Ramah" of Jeremiah is near Bethlehem, in the territory of Benjamin — Rachel's own son's tribal land. In 1 Samuel 10:2, Samuel points Saul to Rachel's tomb as a landmark in Benjamin's territory, confirming the geographic memory of this burial site.
20: "Over her tomb Jacob set up a pillar, and to this day that pillar marks Rachel's tomb" — the third pillar Jacob erects in Genesis (the first was at Bethel in Genesis 28:18; the second at Bethel in Genesis 35:14; this is at Rachel's tomb). The phrase "to this day" (ʿad-hayyôm) is a narrative marker indicating that the pillar was a landmark known to the original audience at the time of writing. The pillar at Rachel's tomb is a memorial that transforms a spot of grief into a covenant landmark — her death, like Abraham's purchase of Machpelah, marks the patriarchal family's claim on Canaan's soil.
21: "Israel moved on again and pitched his tent beyond Migdal Eder" — Migdal Eder (migdal-ʿēḏer, tower of the flock) is a shepherds' watchtower south of Bethlehem, mentioned in Micah 4:8 as a future gathering point for the restored Israel. The stop here — between Rachel's death and the Reuben incident of Genesis 35:22 — is a transitional note marking Jacob's movement southward toward Hebron and Isaac's household.
22: "Reuben went in and slept with his father's concubine Bilhah, and Israel heard of it" — sleeping with a father's concubine or wife was an ANE act of political and social dominance: claiming the subordinate women of a household was a recognized way of asserting claim to the patriarch's position or inheritance. Absalom's sleeping with David's concubines (2 Samuel 16:21-22) was explicitly political — Ahithophel frames it as demonstrating that Absalom's revolt is irreversible. Reuben, as Jacob's firstborn (Genesis 29:32), may be asserting his claim to succession following Rachel's death. Jacob's silence — "Israel heard of it" — without recorded response suggests either emotional exhaustion (he has just buried Rachel) or a deferred judgment (given at his deathbed in Genesis 49:3-4, where Reuben loses his firstborn privileges for this act).
23: "The sons of Leah: Reuben the firstborn of Jacob, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar and Zebulun" — the formal tribal inventory follows immediately after the Reuben incident, a placement that simultaneously lists Reuben as firstborn and contextualizes the incident that will end his firstborn privileges. The enumeration by mother groups mirrors the structure of Numbers 1 (the tribal census) and Deut 33 (the tribal blessings). The six sons of Leah are the largest maternal group, reflecting her greater fertility throughout Genesis 29-30.
24: "The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin" — Rachel's two sons are listed together, united by being the sons of the beloved wife. Joseph (Genesis 30:24) was born in Paddan-Aram during the Laban years; Benjamin was just born here, in Canaan, after Rachel's death. The pairing of Joseph and Benjamin — both born of Rachel, both geographically separated from the other ten brothers' births — prefigures the special relationship between them in the Joseph narrative (Genesis 42:4; Genesis 43:14-16; Genesis 44:20-34).
25: "The sons of Rachel's servant Bilhah: Dan and Naphtali" — the two sons of Rachel's surrogate are listed as Rachel's sons in the tribal enumeration, consistent with the ANE surrogate-mother convention: children born through the maidservant to the mistress's account were legally the mistress's children. Dan and Naphtali appear here as "sons of Rachel" (via surrogate) in the formal list, reflecting their legal status even though Bilhah was their biological mother.
26: "The sons of Leah's servant Zilpah: Gad and Asher. These were the sons of Jacob, born to him in Paddan Aram" — the final summary covers all twelve sons. The concluding note "born to him in Paddan Aram" is technically inaccurate for Benjamin (born in Canaan in Genesis 35:16-18); it likely refers to the majority of the sons or to the formation of the family unit in Paddan-Aram. The twelve-tribe list here is the formal narrative completion of the tribal origin account that began in Genesis 29:32.
27: "Jacob came home to his father Isaac in Mamre, near Kiriath Arba (that is, Hebron), where Abraham and Isaac had stayed" — the return to Isaac closes the Paddan-Aram cycle. Mamre/Hebron is the patriarchal home: Abraham lived at Mamre (Genesis 13:18; Genesis 18:1); Isaac settled at Hebron after the Abimelech period (Genesis 26:23; Genesis 35:27). Jacob's return to his father after twenty years away (Genesis 28:10; Genesis 31:38) is a homecoming that the narrative has anticipated since Jacob's departure. The identification "where Abraham and Isaac had stayed" frames Hebron as the ancestral covenant seat.
28: "Isaac lived a hundred and eighty years" — Isaac's lifespan of 180 years exceeds Abraham's 175 (Genesis 25:7) by five years. The patriarchal lifespans in Genesis follow a descending arc from the antediluvian numbers (Methuselah's 969, Noah's 950) through the early post-flood period to the patriarchs' still-impressive but more bounded centuries. Isaac's 180 years represent a full and complete life in the ANE reckoning — 180 is a highly composite number (60 × 3, 36 × 5) with numerical prestige in ancient Babylonian mathematics.
29: "He breathed his last and died and was gathered to his people, old and full of years. And his sons Esau and Jacob buried him" — the burial formula for Isaac ("gathered to his people") echoes Abraham's death notice (Genesis 25:8) — the standard euphemism for death in Genesis. That both Esau and Jacob buried Isaac echoes Ishmael and Isaac burying Abraham (Genesis 25:9) — in both cases, estranged half-brothers or deceiving brothers are reunited at their father's death to perform the burial. The family burial marks the patriarchal generation's closure: Abraham is already gone; Isaac has now died; Jacob's generation carries the covenant forward.
